title: A Unified Framework for Locally Stable Phases
authors: Zhi Li, Raz Firanko, Timothy H. Hsieh
abstract: We propose a unifying framework for characterizing pure and mixed state phases of matter across equilibrium, non equilibrium, and metastable regimes. We introduce the concept of locally stable states, defined by the operational property that any local operation (including post selection) can be reversed by a local channel. We prove that local stability is equivalent to a state being short range correlated, defined by the decay of both correlations and conditional mutual information. We demonstrate that these properties are invariant under locally reversible channels, thus defining locally stable phases. Furthermore, we prove that local stability implies both the decay of a family of nonlinear correlators, including the fidelity correlator, and the decay of correlations in the canonical purification, thus bridging the gap between mixed and pure states. Along the way, we establish two results which may be of independent interest: we show that post-selection on locally stable (short range correlated) states can be implemented via local channels and that quantum Markov chains can be characterized by the local computability of nonlinear observables.
summary: This paper proposes a new unified framework for characterizing phases of matter by defining 'locally stable states' through the lens of local recoverability. It proves that this operational definition is equivalent to the decay of correlations, effectively bridging the gap between pure and mixed state phase descriptions. The work also introduces a new family of nonlinear observables to diagnose non-equilibrium phases.
main_problem: The lack of a unified framework to characterize phases of matter across equilibrium, non-equilibrium, and metastable regimes, specifically bridging the gap between pure and mixed state descriptions.
main_result: The authors establish that 'local stability' (the ability to reverse local operations via a local channel) is equivalent to 'short-range correlations' (decay of operator correlations and conditional mutual information), providing a unified definition for locally stable phases.
method: The framework utilizes quantum information-theoretic tools including Petz recovery maps, Rényi divergences, and the analysis of quantum Markov chains and Lindbladian dynamics.
model_or_system: Quantum many-body systems on D-dimensional lattices with local Hilbert spaces, including equilibrium Gibbs states, non-equilibrium steady states of local Lindbladians, and systems subject to local decoherence.
key_observables: Operator correlations, conditional mutual information (CMI), fidelity correlators, and a two-parameter family of nonlinear correlators (C_{p,q}).
important_parameters: Correlation length, Markov length, spectral gap of the Lindbladian, and the parameters (p, q) of the nonlinear observables.
main_assumptions: The equivalence between local stability and short-range correlations is subject to technical constraints on the buffer region size and the dimensionality of the lattice.
figures_summary: Figure 1 maps different phase types to diagnostic measures; Figure 2 illustrates lattice partitioning for proofs; Figure 3 shows the schematic for phase invariance; Figure 5 illustrates lattice partitioning for nonlinear observables; Figure 6 shows the light-cone structure of recovery.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of local stability, proves its equivalence to short-range correlations, demonstrates phase invariance under reversible circuits, develops a theory for nonlinear correlators, and concludes with applications to Lindbladian dynamics and counterexamples.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous, unified language for researchers studying open quantum systems and many-body dynamics by linking operational stability (recoverability) to structural properties (correlation decay) across different physical regimes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (left) Summary of main results, which provides a unified framework for the (right) generalized landscape of phases including equilibrium thermal (red) and quantum (blue), and non-equilibrium (green) phase transitions driven by noise, for example. The former can be diagnosed by conventional correlation lengths, for example given by mutual information (MI), and the latter can be diagnosed by conditional mutual information (CMI) and its associated length scale, the Markov length.
FIG. 2. The partition of the lattice used in Theorem 2.

FIG. 3. Schematic proof of Theorem 5: 3a - Locally reversible circuit F : ρ 7→ρ′ and the reverse circuit D . 3b - Recovery channel R′ for ρ′ based on “dressing” the recovery channel R for ρ. 3c - R′ recovers ρ′ from the effect of local operations.
